name: Across the Approach
description: Medic Tova at the Aetherian forward post has eight months of resonance exposure case notes she wants to compare with the Terran medical records — but the only way to make that comparison is through an unofficial carrier willing to cross the approach.
accept_text: She hands you a wrapped packet — thinner than you expected, but she's been precise about what's essential and what isn't. 'Eight months of resonance exposure case notes,' she says. 'Every case I've treated at this post and the two outpost positions I cover during rotation. Name, presenting symptoms, intensity, duration, source exposure where I could determine it. My analysis is at the back.' She pauses. 'The analysis: the exposure cases have increased in frequency and intensity over the eight months, tracking something that I cannot identify from this side of the line. The curve suggests an external variable that's increasing. I have a hypothesis. I don't have the control population to test it.' She meets your eyes. 'The Terran medics near the old chapel position have been working the same territory from the other side. Their case population would be different — different exposure patterns, different baseline health, different Crystal War history — but the underlying variable, if my hypothesis is correct, would be the same variable affecting both populations.' She looks at the packet. 'Her name — I'll know when she gives it. Tell her: it's from the one with the shoulder brace. She'll know who that is.' She pauses. 'And tell her: I think we're both looking at the same thing from different angles, and I think if we combine the views we'll see something neither of us can see alone.'
complete_text: Adra takes the packet and reads the cover page and the analysis without sitting down, which is how she reads things that she doesn't want to take her eyes off. Then she reads through the case files, faster — she's not reading every case, she's reading the methodology and then spot-checking against the shape of her own data. When she looks up her expression has the quality of someone who has just had a conversation they didn't know was possible. 'The same curve,' she says. 'Different populations, same eight-month period, same directional curve.' She closes the packet. 'My analysis has been incomplete because I couldn't identify the external variable. The cases kept increasing and I kept looking for a local explanation — environmental factors at this post, something in the supply chain, something specific to the units rotating through.' She sets the packet down. 'It's not local. It's the same thing from both sides of the line.' She is quiet. 'The Aetherian medic. Tova.' She says the name like someone making a note they're going to keep. 'Tell her: yes to the exchange. Tell her the comparison confirms what neither of us could confirm alone.' She picks up her own case file folder. 'Tell her: I think the external variable is the Grid. I think the Grid's reactivation eight months ago is producing resonance effects in the surrounding landscape that are affecting anyone working in it long-term, and that those effects are accelerating as the Grid stabilizes.' She looks at the packet. 'Tell her I have been treating people for something I couldn't name for eight months. I can name it now. I don't know whether being able to name it helps them — but not being able to was making it worse.'
